entries.intro: Have you ever noticed that bread tastes better when someone else makes it? I can't explain it.
details: I use the same flour, same oven, same recipe. Mine is fine, but theirs is somehow better.
The baker says it's because I'm too used to my own cooking. That doesn't sound like a real answer.
reactions: It bothers me more than it should. I think about it every time I eat.

entries.intro: I found a stone with a perfect hole through the middle the other day. Not cracked. Smooth, like water made it.
details: I've been carrying it in my pocket for a week. No reason. I just like it.
My wife says I'm like a crow, picking up shiny things and bringing them home.
reactions: There's something satisfying about finding a thing like that. Like the ground gave you a little gift.

statCheck.pass: Water bores through soft stone over years. Finds a weak spot and just keeps at it. That hole probably took longer to form than I've been alive.
statCheck.fail: No idea how a hole ends up in a stone like that. Just one of those things.

entries.intro: Why does the cat always sit in the one spot of sunlight? You'd think the whole warm floor would do.
details: I moved a chair into the sunbeam once and the cat sat on the chair. Moved the chair back. Cat went back to the floor.
It's not even a good spot. Right in the middle of the doorway where everyone trips over it.
reactions: I admire the commitment, honestly. That cat knows what it wants.

entries.intro: I've been noticing little things that don't add up if you look closely.
details: Like how things are always a bit different from how I remember them. Small differences. A post that seems taller, a path that curves more.
My neighbor says I'm overthinking it and should find a hobby. He might be right.
reactions: It's probably nothing. But once you start noticing, you can't stop.

statCheck.pass: Memory's unreliable for details like that. Your brain fills in what it expects rather than what's actually there.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why things seem different. Maybe I'm just getting forgetful.

entries.intro: I can never sleep on a full moon. Wide awake, staring at the ceiling every single time.
details: I've tried closing the shutters, covering my head, drinking tea. Nothing works.
My father was the same way. He used to get up and go for walks in the middle of the night.
reactions: I've just accepted it. One bad night a month isn't the worst thing.

statCheck.pass: Could just be the light. Even a little bit coming through the cracks is enough to keep a light sleeper up.
statCheck.fail: Couldn't tell you why it happens. My body just knows.

entries.intro: You ever lose something and find it in a place you already checked? Happened to me twice this week.
details: I lost my good knife and tore the whole house apart. Found it on the table. The first place I looked.
My wife says I don't actually look, I just glance. She's probably got a point.
reactions: It makes me feel like I can't trust my own eyes. Very unsettling.

entries.intro: I've been watching the ants by the front step. They always take the same path, even when there's a shorter one.
details: I put a stick across their trail once to see what would happen. They walked around it and went right back to the old path.
They work harder than anyone I know. No days off, no complaints.
reactions: I could learn something from ants. Or I could just keep watching them instead of working.

statCheck.pass: They follow a scent trail the first ones laid down. It's not about the shortest path. It's the one they all agree on.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they do it. They just seem to know where they're going.

entries.intro: Does soup count as a drink or a food? I asked three people and got three different answers.
details: The baker said food, the old woman said drink, and the carpenter said it depends on how thick it is. That last one bothers me most.
I've been thinking about it for two days. My wife told me to stop.
reactions: These are the questions that keep a person up at night. Or maybe that's just me.
